text: Reactive buyers and documented billing pain point to higher ed and proactive push intelligence—but recovery proof and IT gates must be closed before May.

Primary and secondary research align: commercial energy buyers are reactive, billing errors are structurally real, finance and ops need different surfaces, regulatory intelligence is white space, and Arcadia’s window is real but finite. John and Dan should walk out knowing the beachhead is higher education with Rate Analyst’s regulatory layer as first engineering priority — and that one documented dollar recovered matters more than aggregate identified-error dollars for fundraise and enterprise credibility.

Arcadia’s gap is structural and time-bounded

Arcadia is simultaneously selling new enterprise accounts while migrating existing customers off a legacy Urjanet/Arc platform — job postings confirm this is the Senior PM's primary responsibility. Their attention is divided for 12–24 months.

Has a single dollar actually been recovered?

Confirm SOC 2 status, start Type II (or Type I bridge), and ask pilots early whether IT must approve before data share.
What is the corporate multi-site buyer voice?
Strong higher-ed and channel evidence — but no completed interview with a corporate multi-site energy buyer on paying standalone vs. processor-channel.
Two interviews minimum (one processor-dependent, one not) on willingness to pay for an analytical layer vs. embedding.

Higher education — research universities with district energy, large meter count, EnergyCAP in place, separate finance and ops. Pain, incumbent weakness, calendar, and chargeback moat all align; healthcare / CRE / corporate lack the same combined proof or speed.

Regulatory intelligence inside Rate Analyst (PUC monitoring, dollarized impact, push alerts). Only uncrowded feature with named buyer timing (March budget lock) and no named competitor. Billing detection is the hook but not unique; chargeback is moat, not entry.
12–24 months while Arcadia focuses on Urjanet/Arc migration and corporate dashboard/API posture. Ship HE-specific chargeback and district allocation before that advantage narrows.
If universities won’t pay alongside EnergyCAP: drop replacement framing, sell an intelligence layer on top, and clarify RFP/discretionary-buy path or face 6–12-month procurement drag.
If regulatory intelligence is a delighter, not a trigger: lead with billing errors (with recovery proof) and demote the rate-case story to expansion.
If Arcadia exits migration faster: compress the timeline — start chargeback roadmap by month four, not after pilots.
If Arcadia permanently skips higher ed: pressure on HE may ease, but monitor hires and announcements quarterly — don’t assume.
Do not circulate unaudited aggregate “identified savings” figures externally. Frame detection vs. customer-owned dispute/recovery honestly.
The “80% overpaying” statistic is flagged unverifiable in the claim audit and tied to a vendor with financial interest. Prefer FERC / RI PUC / Harvard enforcement framing.
Do not imply unattended auto-recovery. CC Engine maturity is assists manual completion; claiming otherwise is diligence and misrepresentation risk.
What makes the phone ring for commercial energy teams — and when Clear Current must already be in the room.

Commercial energy managers do not go looking for problems; problems find them, and facilities leaders are buried in day-to-day emergencies rather than proactive monitoring. A product that waits for logins misses the window. This map names the forced-action moments when alerts, dollar impact, and calendar-aware outreach matter most. Triggers cluster by vertical, season, and event type so product push logic and sales timing can align to peak receptivity.
Buyers are pulled into problems; they are not browsing for another dashboard — meet them in forced-action moments.
Login-first products miss the window; push alerts, dollar impact, and calendar-aware outreach win receptivity.
Triggers cluster by vertical, season, and event so product logic and sales timing can align.

Year-round; Jun–Aug and Jan–Feb highest

text: Demand charge disconnected from perceived usage
Aug–Sep post-summer; Feb–Mar post-winter
Most customers don’t know what rate structure they’re on. They don’t know if they have a demand ratchet. They just pay the bill. Even hospitals.
Structural information asymmetry converts when you monetize and explain the mechanism on the bill.
Wrong account, address, or meter attribution
Bills are not always matching the addresses of our sites. Bills for one hospital campus are being paid by another.
Site-to-invoice association is the automation prerequisite for everything else at multi-site scale.
Tax exemption not applied on utility bills
Non-profit tax exemptions — a real billing error category that goes undiscovered for months because nobody is systematically checking the tax line.
Recoverable dollars and credit claims close the loop from identified error to realized savings.
Power-factor penalty without explanation
We’re seeing power factor readings of 79–81% at one of our campuses. Below where we should be — but I’m not sure if it’s equipment or meter malfunction.
Even sophisticated buyers need a fast second opinion separating billing error from operations.
Ratchet locks after unexpected demand event
Aug–Sep after spike; cost persists 12 months
If a customer hits a high demand in July, they may pay for that demand for the next 12 months. Most don’t know until the bill is already high.
High-dollar and poorly understood. Modeling and warnings beat post-bill surprise.
Utility dispute with no historical documentation
We had someone doing our bill processing for 10 to 15 years and she recently left, so we’re scrambling a little.
Archived bills and patterns are the evidence base for disputes — and continuity after turnover.
Retail supplier transition billing overlap or gap
Contract anniversaries; Q4 budget cycle
ERCOT has 100+ REPs, an 87% commercial switching rate, and overlapping billing cycles during supplier transitions.
Structural complexity in home markets like ERCOT is a repeatable wedge for transition-error detection.
TOU tier misclassification on the bill
Year-round; costliest in summer peak
On-peak, off-peak, discounted off-peak, super off-peak, extreme on-peak — all actual names. Wrong tier and nobody catches it without line-by-line review.
Automated tariff cross-check beats manual workbook lookup at scale.
Water or wastewater consumption anomaly
Summer irrigation; leaks year-round
They listed 35,000 gallons instead of the typical 3,500 gallons.
Multi-utility anomaly detection expands the platform beyond electrons alone.
Supply contract 9–12 months from expiration
The contract renewal window is the moment when customers actually pay attention to their energy costs.
Known calendar event with a finite decision window suits Deal Center + renewal alerts.
Higher-ed Jan–Mar; corporate Q3–Q4
Our budget is submitted in March, but the City of Austin’s rate changes are often not finalized until after we’ve already submitted. So we’re guessing.
Budget-vs-actual and rate intelligence close the gap finance cannot defend without data.
Natural gas purchasing window (spring)
We buy $20 million in natural gas every year plus or minus depending on pricing. That’s a proactive decision we make in the spring.
Large commodity decisions need baseline data and structured comparison in a narrow season.
New fiscal year reset and reconciliation
Our fiscal year is September 1 through August 31. That’s when everything resets — budgets, building assignments, everything.
Portfolio onboarding and prior-year actuals anchor the new-year baseline.
Scheduled generator test risks summer peak
We test emergency generators at 80% load bank during summer — required for accreditation. But we’re not always thinking about what that does to our peak demand.
Proactive calendar insert prevents a scheduled event from becoming a 12-month ratchet problem.

Seasonal Energy Management Calendar
Commercial energy work clusters into predictable seasonal beats — budget, procurement, summer demand, compliance — so GTM should ride that rhythm, not run flat outreach.

Energy managers face recurring fiscal cliffs: budgets and forecasts lock while utility rates, capacity charges, and contract terms are still moving. That timing gap — especially before March budget submission for many schools and before October healthcare fiscal years — creates recurring moments when invoice-level intelligence and regulatory foresight are most valuable.
Work clusters into predictable seasonal beats — budget, procurement, summer demand, compliance — not flat year-round urgency.
Forecasts often freeze before rates, capacity charges, and contract terms finish moving — that gap is the leverage point.
March (many schools) and October (healthcare FY) are recurring cliffs for invoice intelligence and regulatory foresight.
GTM should ride the calendar rhythm instead of steady generic outreach.

Five reactive workflows where late discovery, manual multi-source investigation, and brittle resolution leave teams flying blind until dollars and credibility are already lost.

Each map is built from primary interviews first and secondary research second, with product stages naming Clear Current surfaces (Rate Analyst, Energy HQ, Deal Center) and tiers (Dashboard push, Chat). The recurring pattern is backward-looking, threshold-based detection that misses baseline drift, expertise concentrated in people who may leave, and resolution that is political, slow, and often partial — while CC’s honest position is spot-and-assist with human-in-the-loop completion.
Built interviews-first: stages name real CC surfaces (Rate Analyst, Energy HQ, Deal Center) and push vs chat tiers.
Status quo is backward-looking, threshold-based detection — easy to miss baseline drift until money is already gone.
Investigation is multi-source and expert-heavy; resolution is often political, slow, and partial.
Honest CC posture: spot-and-assist with human-in-the-loop completion — not autonomous recovery promises.
Commercial energy manager at a multi-site institution reviewing many meters per cycle.
Andee Chamberlain · Sean Sevy · John Culver
Monthly utility bills arrive as a high-volume mix of formats, cycles, and providers across the portfolio.
Invoices arrive via mail, email, or portals. Tools are backward-looking and deviation-based.
Threshold rules miss errors that have become the new baseline. Comprehensive review is impossible at scale.
Teams pull history, reconcile units, and cross-check meters, brokers, and utilities when something flags.
Attribution and format chaos make each invoice a multi-system, expert-heavy slog with no guaranteed resolution.
Confirmed errors require utility contact, documentation, credits, and internal escalation.
Identified errors often diverge from realized recovery. Politics and time swallow outcomes.
CC surface: Rate Analyst — Dashboard + Chat
Ingest and associate invoices to sites/meters; push ranked anomalies; NL investigation with baselines and annotated history; generate an evidence package for disputes.
Does not autonomously file disputes or guarantee credits. Resolution timeline and utility dynamics stay human. Not every ingestion/association edge case is solved.
Energy operations manager responding to spikes or broken consumption patterns.
James Norton · Sean Sevy · Intermountain Health team
A meter spikes, a bill shows an inexplicable number, or a pattern breaks from historical shape — often noticed late or by accident.
Detection is periodic review, threshold rules, or non-energy staff noticing a big number.
Late, accidental discovery lets losses accumulate. Baseline drift and shared-meter blind spots evade rules.
Triage spans bills, interval data, work orders, utilities, and brokers to separate billing vs equipment vs ops causes.
Non-linear, multi-source work with unit and domain mismatches. Hard to monetize the finding for the org.
Path splits across utility disputes, facilities / capital work, or policy — often needing finance and ops alignment.
Correct diagnosis still hits politics, capital gates, and hesitation to escalate without airtight proof.
CC surface: Energy HQ + Rate Analyst — Dashboard primary, Chat for triage
Portfolio anomaly alerts prioritized by dollar impact. NL triage and annotated history. Evidence packaging and financial framing for escalation.
Does not complete utility negotiation, capital approval, or org politics. Sub-hour real-time BAS monitoring is not described as shipped — alerts tie to invoice-level data.
Campus Chargeback & Departmental Billing
University utility billing manager allocating real utility costs across departments and auxiliaries.
Period close forces internal allocation of paid utility costs as the campus footprint and metering reality keep shifting.
Mixed metering and district-energy complexity collide with tools that promised more integration than they deliver.
Data for usage-based chargeback is missing or siloed. Monthly reconciliation is manual on a deadline.
Teams disaggregate central plant costs, convert units, and defend assumptions ops and finance each need differently.
Dual-audience tension — usage vs dollars — with no system that serves both. Iterative approximations get contested.
Charges go out; departments accept or dispute under square-footage or weak signals.
Charges feel arbitrary, so conservation incentives fail. Disputes stay painful to defend with assembled evidence.
CC surface: Rate Analyst + Energy HQ — Dashboard + Chat
Stronger meter-to-building association, validation, anomaly flags, building-level consumption and cost views, and role-shaped NL queries from one dataset.
Does not replace a full chargeback/ERP system. District-energy disaggregation, departmental allocation logic, and deep ERP integration are roadmap-level for this vertical wedge.
Regulatory Intelligence & Budget Impact
Energy manager or finance director exposed when rate cases land after budgets are set.
Budget uses forward rate assumptions; later, utilities finalize increases on a regulatory calendar misaligned with institutional budgeting.
Dockets are public but dense; in practice people learn from bills, irregular notices, brokers, or luck.
The mismatch stays invisible until variance hits. No bridge between PUC process and budget calendar.
Finance models dollar impact across schedules and buildings; ops and finance wrestle the same data in different shapes.
Manual rate-schedule mapping and updates make precise, timely portfolio math expensive.
Teams update budgets or absorb variance and rarely execute proactive mitigation in the reactive scramble.
Mitigation and class-review opportunities are missed. Explanations to leadership stay technical and slow.
CC surface: Rate Analyst — Dashboard + Chat
Vision in the map: docket-aware alerts with portfolio impact, instant scenario Q&A, and CFO-ready variance memos tied to tariff logic.
Automated PUC docket monitoring integrated with impact modeling is explicitly not described as shipped. Tariff/scenario engine exists, but the monitoring/intake layer is the roadmap extension.
Budgeting & Forecasting (Annual Cycle)
Energy and finance leaders building a defensible annual energy budget and multi-year view.
Andi Ault · Andee Chamberlain · Phil Combs
Annual budget season starts — energy is a scrutinized line item amid rising costs and opaque drivers.
Teams blend last-year actuals with escalation guesses and parallel finance assumptions.
Rate-case timing vs budget lock guarantees structural error. Risk stays hidden until variance reports.
Forecasting splits into volume, rate, and schedule/contract mix — usually only partially supported by tools.
Rate path is the broken component. Optimization never gets attention during the crunch.
Budget submits with known weak assumptions. Later variance erodes credibility and capital asks.
Wrong for structural reasons outside the energy manager’s control still burns organizational trust.
CC surface: Rate Analyst + Deal Center — Dashboard + Chat
Budget-open briefings from monitored portfolio context; NL scenario modeling for finance and ops; gas/strip context alongside consumption via Deal Center; documented assumptions for CFO questions.
PUC monitoring layer remains roadmap. Full M&V-grade baselines and ranked multi-year capital ROI are directional roadmap — not fully shipped as described.
Every journey starts reactive. Stage 1 is routinely late discovery, and stress peaks in investigation and resolution — not always at the trigger.
A single structural theme repeats: flying blind until problems surface with accumulated time and dollars lost.
Expertise and memory concentrated in one long-tenured person creates brittle detection that disappears when they leave.
The dual-audience fracture (ops wants usage; finance wants dollars) runs across higher ed and large commercial accounts.
Three CC strengths span the maps: proactive dollar-ranked alerts, NL investigation on shared data, and monetized CFO-ready outputs.
The honest cross-cutting hole is identified-vs-realized recovery. Faster evidence does not equal guaranteed refunds or credits.
